content.text: The same Bloomtusk is worth 800 to the right knife and 200 to the wrong one.

The Carver owns yield and markets — what the kill is worth, and who pays full price for it. The trade bleeds from Harvesting: anyone can field-dress a kill on a Harvesting check; the Carver's cuts sell. The field is the office — Carvers level on mission, knife in hand.

content.title: Carver
content.subtitle: Yield & markets
content.desc: PP economy, tiers, and the One Name rule live on Professions Overview.

content.text: The Knife Trade (entry, 4 PP): opens the trade — the Work verb and the field verb are yours.
Buyer's List (T2, 4 PP): best buyer and current prices, always.
Cold Lockers (T2, 5 PP + 400 cr): preservation clocks run ×2 in your kit (the clocks live on Carving).
Grade Stamp (T2, 6 PP): your goods sell +20% — the stamp means something. Obligation: your name is now policeable, and counterfeits exist.
Offal Economy (T2, 5 PP): nothing wastes — every carve yields a bonus reagent lot.
Premium Channel (T3, 7 PP): graft-viable organ buyers, full price. Obligation: those buyers know your route.
anyone paying full price for organs already knows what yours are worth. walk a different route home.
Trophy Rights (T3, 6 PP): named kills yield a unique trophy component — crafting and graft seeds.
Carver's Name (T3, 8 PP): standing corp harvest commissions, one tier above rep.

What a carve yields — by body taxonomy, with the Harvesting Check and the three clocks (mission time, preservation, Capacity) — lives on Carving. The Carver's perks change what those yields are worth, not what they are.
Graft-viable organs are premium contraband; the biology lane lives on Mutations & Grafts.
The cargo chain: carving creates cargo, cargo needs the van, and the van needs the Rigger — Capacity and transport live on Vehicles (see Rigger).

Lot inspected. Grade confirmed. The stamp on this consignment has been verified against the registry — counterfeit marks are prosecuted on behalf of the holder, whether or not the holder survives them.
